item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images used in this article are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image is from the 15th century as stated here and thus is Public Domain, and the rest of the images are just text, symbols, and another public domain image, indicated here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final table can be made noticeably shorter and less repetitive by removing the "recorded as Observer####" lines from nearly every entry. Those bits are redundant with the numbers given in the first column.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This left me looking for further hints that #12 might be Pope Francis. Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the building appears to always be present, it is not directly observable. Subjects pointed in the direction of the building fail to notice its presence.

Isn't it called "antimemetics"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The highlight was the pamphlet, everything else was just middling, from the non-descriptive description to the bloated experiment log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fun because it mocks catholicism. I actually love it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic; I think that "not-humans trying to mimic human activities" is one of my favorite SCP types. However, the xeroxed pamphlet was what really made it for me.

"[S]uccessfully prayed everything but his feet out of hell" shall forevermore be one of my favorite SCP lines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing I recognize I should say something about for the sake of my obsessiveness - MRI scan is a poor choice for follow up on stomach cancer, unless it metastasized to brain or bone. CT fits better for the disease.

Also, you wrote "there is does ~" in the beginning of first sentence of Exhaustive Research. Might wanna erase 'is'

PS
Unificationist! As a Korean I don't know if I should be surprised or embarrassed someone outside Korea knows that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I been had; I took this entirely as straight-forward and not until re-reading after the comments realized this was a scam

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Links to the pamplets are broken, FYI.

I have to wonder if there's some mental compulsion going on with how its getting peoples money if they're just good at targeting those who would give up their cash. Either way, enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: upvoted for "preserved in out reliquaries" they dont even give efforts XD. unless that was unintentional, then shame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Regarding the numerous remarks against Jews, could a Jewish test subject possibly view (and maybe even enter) the building? What reactions would be seen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If his feet cost about $7…

$84 or so is quite the allowance for a five year old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea that they are really bad at scamming people, despite all the effort they put into it. 15000 lira is/was about $7.00…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is brilliant. In basically every way. The containment procedures are maybe a tiny bit sparse (how exactly are they containing a building in the middle of Rome? Just standing outside it going 'No entry!' when people approach?) but apart from that… yeah. The 5 year old test subject was a bit 'woah, what are you doing, Foundation?' (in a good way).

Surprised there's no mention of an attempt to send someone in all wired up, but I imagine it wouldn't have worked anyway. Also, the disappearing 10 year old felt like an old, cheap, Catholics-are-paedophiles gag. Which was never really that funny anyway.

item.title: Slow clap
item.content:encoded: Just…… damn. Good job on the pamphlet, while I know it was intentional, I'm still a little iffy about the foundation using a 5 year old with no audio-visual equipment to talk with entities of unknown hostile potential, but still. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am on the fence. On one hand, there is every indicator that these are scammers, and scammers who just don't get it. On the other hand, it's still mocking the faith with caricatures. I guess that, to me, it's like an alien showing up with exaggerated blackface characteristics and trying to collect money for NAACP. Sure, it's obviously got it all wrong, but that still doesn't make it any less offensive, you know?

item.title: I was on the fence a while
item.content:encoded: …but as a Norwegian: Observer-1521-15 meant it had to be a upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but the execution bugs me in vague ways that I can't fully express. So I'm just going to re-read and spitball stuff as I see it:
-The picture. It makes sense to use a sketch, given the perception anomaly. But why is the Foundation using it as an "is this what you saw?" when they have photos from observer 10?
-Why is monitor duty here so terrible that it requires such frequent rotation? I would think a safe, boring job watching a building that is sort-of not really there would be a plum assignment; used as a reward for excellent performance. Sort of the opposite of Keter duy.
-"…not directly observable." Needs some sort of qualifier. Because it is directly observable, just not by the general populace. One of the selected can directly observe it all they care to, it's the basis of the containmetn procedures.
-Does the buiding not exist at all for non-affected? Can an observer lean up against it while a non-observer walks right thru? This is an important detail of the anomaly, and something that the Foudnation would test and take note of.
-"…period garb…" Which period? Baroque presumably, to go wtih the architecture, but it's important to be clear.
-Why haven't they tried to learn more about the entities inside? At least an attempt to have one fo the observers lure or force a nun/priest outisde would seem in order.
-The entities attempt to defuse threats of violence, but what do they do if actually harmed? I can see not testing this, because it might rile up a currently passive anomaly, but maybe a rejected testign proposal?
-It is never good form to presume. How about, "All individuals capable of observing the building have also been capable of entry when tested." or some-such. The point is to re-word it as a statement of observed facts, and not as a presumptive conclusion.
-Wait, what happened to the rest of the description? The description is supposed to tell me all the important broad facts of what this is and what it does, addenda are for additional detail and flavor. All this description gives me is a building that is only there for soem people, which is fairly 'meh'.
-There needs to be something heavier pointing towards the pamphlets. They are the best part of this and do most of the explaining. But it never would have occured to me to click on them if I hadn't seen the clue in the comments.
-"While the original has been lost…" this detail is not only unnecessary, it is damaging. The Foundation just misplacing their only concrete piece of evidence relating to an anomaly is gross incompetence of an unbelievable degree. It's the sort of casual idiocy that makes people hate lolfoundation.

That's enough wall-o-critique out of me. Not like any of it amounts to much stacked agaisnt a +64 anyway, but there's my opinion all the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Problem is still there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this. It is like delicious candy. Especially the pamphlets. And all the other accurate and accidentally-inaccurate religious reference. And those fucking pamphlets. Did I mention the pamphlets?

A+++ would upvote again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the typos myself. I hope the author doesn't mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A SCP of Catholic scammers? Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A 10-year-old missing, strange.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if it is a bit of a hassle to read at some points, I think it's well worth it. Good stuff. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1521-HI1 was provided with a short explanation of what a bank was, then given a pamphlet

From the context, it is the observer who is provided with the explanation and given the pamphlet; HI1 provides and gives.

In total, Observer-1521-2 noted seventeen total figures.

It total total.

had successfully prayed everything but his feet out of hell, and an additional 15000 lira to finish.

Looks like there's a word missing here.

There is does not seem

No-vote. This is just not my kind of skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even the D-class thinking that his cancer was cured, no really I am, isn't anomalous. It's a variant on faith healing and wish fulfillment fantasies, wherein the ill person wants to be cured so very, very much that when someone says that God (or reiki or homeopathy or crystal healing or whatever) fixed them, the placebo effect kicks in long enough for pain symptoms to abate briefly, which they take as proof that the "treatment" actually worked. And then they refuse to believe anything to the contrary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1521 is assigned a minimum of two “observers” and three additional support staff working in twenty-four hour shifts.

Twenty-four hour shifts? Why not twelve or eight hour shifts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that the Foundation would have tried to:

1) Have an observer lead a non-observer by the hand into the building.

2) Wire the observer up for audio and video surveillance before sending him/her in.

Of course, it'd be fine for these things to not work, but I'd think the Foundation would at least try.

While the building appears to always be present, it is not directly observable.

It seems to me that it can be directly observed, but only by certain people.

No data suggesting a pattern of who was capable of observing SCP-1521 and why is available.

Seems to me that there is a pattern: everyone with an identified religion was a monotheist. There were no atheists, no agnostics, no polytheists, no pantheists, etc.

EDIT: Also, none of the observers were Jews, either religiously or ethnically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The exploration logs are a bit of a chore to read, what with all the numbers being tossed around, and all I got out of the final addendum is that Observer 13 is 5 years old, which makes his log extremely worse (effectively so).

Otherwise, though, I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, although I did feel it dragged on. My main quibble was that the most interesting part of the article was in the first half, leaving the rest to kind of go downhill. Very nice though. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is, he mentioned it below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not the author, obviously, but I read it to be intentional, since they're scam artists or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
they do demonstrate a bunch of other powers

Really? They can hide the building from the perceptions of most people…. they can affect most people's perceptions of the language in which the sign is written… they can affect people's perceptions of photos of the building… basically, there's nothing anomalous that doesn't involve fucking with people's perceptions.

The stuff with the fountain - that's basically a Jacuzzi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, but they do demonstrate a bunch of other powers. It's not too much of a stretch for them to have healing in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted at "The Bones of Saint John the Baptist as an Infant", futilely upvoted again at "we regret that blessings cannot be sent through the mail", and I'm not even finished reading yet, gloria, gloria, gloria, forever and ever, amen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're just scammers. Elaborate scammers, yes, but scammers, They have no influence on cancer whatsoever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interdimensional strippers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost feel like they should be more effective at the cancer thing. Or are they just not powerful enough to fix that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both corrected. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think I found a typo:

1521-HI14 inquired as to the nature of Observer-1521-13's visit, at which time he was informed that Observer-1521-11 was requesting a healing miracle.

Is this intented?

Also:

Upon entering, he was immediately approached by a humanoid figure wearing a priests smock and robes, designated SCP-1521 Humanoid Instance 46 (1521-HI47).

So… which is it?

These are tiny nit-picks. I +1'd the SCiP, as I really like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not just them, I think. That the Foundation is willing to use a five year old like that (no idea where they got him from, but it does say he's a Foundation test subject) says a lot about it as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That one is intentional. And I think it puts these guys into perspective better than anything else in the SCP, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just noticed something, Troy. It says that Addendum SCP-1521-3 was viewed by Observer 1521-13. Now, it says that 13 went in there alone, talked to the priests, payed them some money, and tried to get some more. Thing is, the lists says that 13 is five years old. Now, this might be intentional, but it does seem rather odd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I named the final log "Exhaustive Research Materials Included for Voluntary Perusal" in an attempt to indicate that it wasn't necessary reading. There's a couple of neat things in there, but for the most part, it's fine to skip it.

And I've got no idea what they're doing with the money. Neither does the Foundation. Probably something naughty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. :) Thanks, Aelanna!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed it.

Originally, the last log was twenty-seven entries long. It was an attempt to show that the Foundation had seriously attempted to zero in on the one, truly anomalous thing about these guys: the perception issue. Cutting people from the log led to tons and tons of weird issues throughout the SCP, including the omission of an addendum I really liked, but it made the thing much more readable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was kinda hoping I would come to the discussion page and find that somehow this was more than just the scam artists that I thought it was.

I really am not that interested by this, and the last log dragged on too long to hold my interest. Also, what exactly are these entities doing with the money? Like… do they just keep it? To have? Or are they able to leave and spend it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Drew is catching what I was going for. A 15th century Catholic also wouldn't have called God "Jehova." ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, that makes sense. And explains the rest.

"Bones of St. John the Baptist as an infant!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That could certainly be the case. If it is, they should probably pray (heh) that someone like the Horizon Initiative doesn't lay their hands on them. That could go very badly indeed for our scammers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stacked the images vertically because the page looks horrendous for anyone viewing it at a lower resolution. Feel free to revert if you really, really don't like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The drawing caption says it was made by Observor -8, but that's the old guy who had a heart attack and died upon viewing 1521. Was that supposed to be drawn by a different Observor?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy Bast mother of cats. I loved this even without the pamphlets, (I upvoted when I read about them praying all but his uncle's feet out of hell, which'd require additional 15 000 lira) but those are plain awesome. Bloody hell, *this* is how to do a religious SCP right.

- - -
Jesus Saves! . .. in the Most Holy Bank of his Holiness Pope Leo the Tenth, Saint in the Waiting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My interpretation is that not only are they scammers, but they're not even very good scammers. They're from… elsewhere/when, and just using the trappings of Catholicism/Leo X as a front. Like the Nigerian Prince scams. And having not researched Leo X in great detail, they tossed in Zionism as "you know, something the Catholics hated back then, right?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very amusing, and I enjoyed the various historical jabs in it (such as the hatred for German speakers, which makes sense considering Leo X was the Pope during the Reformation). The dig at the practice of Indulgence was also appreciated. The only thing I would comment on are the various jabs at Jew/Zionist. As Orion mentioned above the word Zionist was not used at the time, and it is also notable that Leo X was not particularly antisemitic at all. In fact, he borrowed money from Jewish loaners himself, and indeed allowed them several liberties uncommon at the time, and I quote:

"Leo X abolished certain discriminatory levies, did not enforce the wearing of the badges Jews had been forced to put on in the 12th century and also sanctioned the establishment of the Hebrew Printing Press. Leo X, as well as other popes from this period, such as Sixtus IV, retained Jewish physicians in Rome." (Rebecca Weiner, Jewish Virtual Library)

Other than that, a damn good SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting, upgraded to 'Very Interesting' once revealed that the "miracles" don't actually heal people.

Minor quibble: The word "Zionist" didn't exist until the 19th Century. Since the rest of the article is stuck in a 15th Century mindset, this contrast is jarring for people who care far too much about history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interdimensional angelic Catholic scam artists.

Interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got it! Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit of a booboo in the second addendum. The observer is initially referred to as 13 and then is called 11 for the rest of it.
